item.title: Cisco SD-WAN CVE-2026-20245 Zero-Day Exploited; Netskope Warns on Shadow AI Risks
item.content:encoded:
Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Zero-Day CVE-2026-20245 Exploited to Gain Root Access — Must Read

Mandiant says an unknown actor exploited Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN flaw CVE-2026-20245 as a zero-day to escalate from a compromised admin account to root access at a communications service provider. The attackers used stealthy anti-forensic steps, including restoring changed passwords and deleting evidence, highlighting the risk of zero-day exploitation on edge network devices.

  • Mandiant reported that an unknown threat actor exploited Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN vulnerability CVE-2026-20245 as a zero-day at least two months before public disclosure.
  • CVE-2026-20245 allows an authenticated local attacker with netadmin privileges to execute arbitrary commands with elevated privileges by supplying a crafted file to an affected system.
  • The incident targeted an unspecified communications service provider and involved elevating a compromised admin account to full root-level access.
  • Mandiant observed two distinct periods of unauthorized activity, one between late 2025 and January 2026 and another in March 2026, but it is unclear whether the two events are connected.
  • The attacker used anti-forensic techniques including deleting files, reversing configuration changes, restoring a changed admin password, and running a validation script to confirm indicators were gone.

AI Turned The Patch Window Into The Threat Window — Optional

AI is making vulnerability discovery and exploitation far faster than organizations can patch, turning the traditional patch window into an AI-speed threat window. The key takeaway is to stop relying on patching alone and prioritize data-centric controls, zero trust access, and visibility into AI apps and agents to limit what attackers can reach before fixes land.

  • Anthropic’s Project Glasswing reportedly surfaced 10,000 high- or critical-severity vulnerabilities across critical enterprise software in a single month.
  • The article says the median time to remediate a high-severity vulnerability is two weeks, while AI can reduce work such as mapping an environment, chaining attack paths, and developing an exploit from weeks to minutes.
  • The author argues that organizations should not focus only on patching faster, but should control what can be reached while waiting for patches to land.
  • The article states that 98% of organizations report unmanaged AI use, only 12% can detect all shadow AI activity, and 91% cannot reliably stop a risky AI-driven action before it executes.
  • The article cites CVE-2025-32711 as a zero-click prompt injection in Microsoft 365 Copilot that used a crafted email to extract data from OneDrive, SharePoint, and Teams and exfiltrate it through a trusted Microsoft domain.

The Parable of the Bullet Holes: AI Security Edition — Optional

The article argues that AI security teams risk survivorship bias by focusing only on issues visible in sanctioned enterprise LLMs while missing bigger risks from public LLM use, shadow AI, and unmonitored SaaS AI. Organizations should prioritize finding and controlling these unseen AI data exposure paths.

  • The article uses Abraham Wald’s World War II analysis of bomber bullet holes as an analogy for survivorship bias in AI security.
  • Organizations may focus on minor policy violations and sanitized PII found in monitored enterprise LLM systems while concluding that their systems are secure.
  • The article identifies public LLM use by employees and unapproved cloud LLM instances created by development teams as unmonitored AI risks.
  • Sensitive customer PII and proprietary source code can be exposed outside enterprise monitoring systems, leaving major failures invisible to audits.
  • The article argues that AI security efforts should focus on finding unmonitored services, embedded AI in SaaS, shadow AI, and personal public LLM accounts.
